raw_consumer_statement:
On XX/XX/year>, I placed an order totaling {$560.00} on XXXX official website. XXXX tracking showed the package as delivered on XX/XX/XXXX, but I never received the merchandise. I contacted XXXX on XX/XX/XXXX to request a refund or reshipment, but they refused. I then filed a dispute with American Express the same day. 

On XX/XX/XXXX, Amex denied my dispute, citing that the merchant had provided a delivery photo from XXXX. I submitted a written request to reopen the dispute, explaining that the photo does not depict my residence and that I never received the package. 

However, on XX/XX/XXXX, Amex rejected my request to reopen the dispute, again using the exact same reasoning that the XXXX delivery photo proves the item was delivered, and claiming that I failed to provide evidence of non-receipt. 

I want to clearly state : A XXXX delivery scan or photo does not constitute conclusive proof that I personally received the package. Amex rebilled me within XXXX hours without contacting me, investigating further, or reviewing the documentation I had offered to submit. This is an irresponsible and one-sided decision, failing to conduct a fair investigation and clearly siding with the merchant without protecting the rights of the consumer. 

I also want to ask : How can a customer reasonably provide proof that they did not receive a package? The burden of proof should not fall entirely on the consumer, especially when the merchants only evidence is a generic delivery photo that doesnt match my property. 

I am exercising my legal right as a cardholder to dispute a transaction for goods I paid for but never received. According to Amexs own policy, You can dispute a charge if you havent received the goods or services. I respectfully request that CFPB assist in holding American Express accountable for proper dispute handling and ensure that my case is fairly reviewed.
